pages.text: Mechanical and durability properties of concrete made with dredged marine sand J. Limeira ⇑, M. Etxeberria, L. Agulló, D. Molina Department of Construction Engineering, Polytechnic University of Catalonia, Jordi Girona, 1-3, C1 Barcelona 08034, Spain article info Article history: Received 3 November 2010 Received in revised form 15 April 2011 Accepted 19 April 2011 Available online 17 May 2011 Keywords: Dredged marine sand Volume of permeable pores Mechanical properties Durability properties abstract The process of depletion of sources of natural aggregates challenges the production of technically and environmentally adequate concrete. Alternative material from marine sources, especially for concrete maritime structures, could represent an acceptable solution for this problem and it might also be of great interest for port management. This research work describes the study of the mechanical and durability properties of concretes fabricated with dredged marine sand (DMS) as a ﬁne granular corrector in partial substitution of raw sand (from 15% to up to 50% by raw sand mass) designed for harbor pavements. Three DMS samples were extracted from the Port of Barcelona. The material was stockpiled in the open air and no washing, drying or decontamination process was carried out. Mineralogical, physical and chemical properties of DMS material were determined. Eight different mixtures were produced incorporating three types of DMS material as granular corrector in partial substitution of crushed limestone raw sand. The concretes were submitted to compressive strength tests after 7 and 28 days of moist curing, as well as density, absorption, accessible pores, elastic modulus, tensile and splitting tensile strength, abrasion, cap- illary suction, water penetration under pressure and ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV) tests, all of them after 28 days of moist curing. This study shows that dredged marine sand can be successfully used as a ﬁne aggregate for concrete production. This is justiﬁed by the similar physical and mechanical proper- ties of concrete made with DMS comparing to reference concrete. It was veriﬁed that the use of DMS in substitution of raw sand maintained or reduced the accessible pores, the sorptivity and the water pene- tration depth under pressure. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Most of the aggregates used in the manufacture of concrete come from quarries or alluvial rivers. Nowadays, these sources of natural aggregates are in the process of depletion and their extrac- tion also has harmful consequences for the environment. For these reasons, it is important to optimize the consumption of natural aggregates as well as to enhance their replacement by other alter- native sources. This research will examine the potential use of ﬁne marine aggregates resulting from maintenance dredging activities as substitutes for natural aggregates for the manufacture of con- cretes for maritime structures. Marine aggregates offer an easier transport and the possibility of combining the desired size frac- tions, which can be a considerable advantage in front of land-based aggregates. In this sense, maritime structures such as pavements represent a good alternative for technical, economic and environ- mental solutions using this kind of material. However, special attention must be paid since a harsh environment and a durability requirement for long live service are indispensable [1] for these structures. In recent years

Barrier, London’s National Theater, or the Tamar Bridge of Plymouth (UK). Outside Europe there are also remarkable constructions like the artiﬁcial island of Chek Lap Kok, where the Hong Kong Airport is located, or the Palm Islands in Dubai [12]. According to Gutt and Collins [13], the geological origin of mar- ine aggregates is similar to that of the aggregates commonly used in the manufacture of concrete. In fact, natural or raw aggregates were submerged due to rising sea levels since the ice age. The main differences detected between the raw aggregates and those from marine dredging are the presence of salts, shells and possible con- tent of organic matter in the latter ones. These aspects must be controlled if they are used as constituents of concrete, because in excessive amounts they can trigger the development of different pathologies such as corrosion of the reinforcement or increase of permeability. The evaluation must be carried out taking into ac- count the properties of the aggregates with respect to the require- ments for the particular use to which they will be subjected in the concrete. Size fraction is usually the main property used for DMS characterization. A high proportion of dredged sand usually pre- sents a fraction of material that passes through the 125 lm sieve. However, ﬁne particle content changes with location and time. With respect to the salt content, aggregates from marine dredg- ing have the same chlorides and sulfates than seawater. Moreover, due to the proximity to harbor areas where dredging tasks are per- formed, it is necessary to consider an additional kind of pollution such as heavy metals. In the United States, studies [14] carried out on dredged aggregates from different harbors have concluded that there were low concentrations of heavy metals in them (lead, chromium, mercury, arsenic, cadmium), as well as PCB’s and cya- nide. It is important to note that the ﬁne size fraction of marine aggregates and the presence of contaminants play an important role, since the dimension of the sediments is directly related to the latter composites: the smaller the grains (<63 lm), the higher the presence of contaminants. Several European countries have their respective standard pol- icies of marine aggregates with respect to contamination and dis- posal. The EC itself has no speciﬁc limits for contaminant levels in dredged material, but a summary of various countries’ action levels for heavy metals can be found in the literature [15]. If con- centrations of these substances exceed the limits, a treatment is re- quired. Some decontamination techniques like the stabilization of heavy metals and organic thermal elimination are often used [16]. Other treatments that have been successfully developed are the chemical treatment, the heat treatment and the pulverization treatment. A Columbia University Treatment (CUT) is described in a US patent application [17] seeking to reduce the contaminants of dredged material which are encapsulated or permanently immobilized. The treatment is based on the physicochemical reor- ganization of the micro-structure of the dredged material. This procedure was adopted in a research [18] about the beneﬁciation of dredged material from the Port of New York and New Jersey. According to the author, destruction of the conglomerates in dredged material is necessary prior to or during detoxiﬁcation, as in the case of heavy metals or other inorganic toxic elements. In general, the main goals o

determine the physical, mechanical and durability properties of concrete made with DMS. The test results were compared with those obtained with conventional concrete. 2. Materials and experimental program 2.1. Materials Rapid-hardening type II Portland cement CEM II/A-M 42.5 R for exposure to the maritime environment according to UNE-EN 197-1 speciﬁcations was used for con- crete production. Tables 1 and 2 show the cement characteristics and its chemical composition, respectively. Three fractions of crushed limestone raw aggregates were considered: ﬁne aggregate FA1 0/4 mm; coarse aggregate CA1 4/12 mm and CA2 12/20 mm. Three DMS from the Port of Barcelona (here named DMS-A, DMS- B and DMS-C) were used in partial substitution of FA1. In order to achieve the same required workability in all concretes, a plasticizer additive was used. 2.1.1. Physical and chemical characterization of raw aggregates and DMS Physical properties like size fraction, density and absorption of each DMS were obtained as the average of three values. The samples for testing were obtained by sampling 1 m 3 of each DMS extracted from two stockpiles of the harbor site. In or- der to determine possible contaminant components, exhaustive sampling was car- ried out according to the UNE-EN 932-1 speciﬁcation. Table 3 indicates the density and absorption of the aggregates used in this study. They were obtained according to UNE-EN 1097-6 speciﬁcations. Size fraction was determined in accordance with UNE-EN 933-1 (see Fig. 1 ). According to the sieve analysis, DMS-A showed a ﬁner size fraction (0/1 mm) with more than 50% passing through a 125 lm sieve, in front of DMS-B (0/4 mm) and DMS-C (0/0.5 mm) which ranged 7%. Fine content was also higher in DMS-A with 14% of the material passing through a 63 lm sieve, while DMS-B and DMS-C showed a lower content of this fraction. The reason for this might be the fact that the DMS- A samples were obtained from the surface layer of the disposal site at the Port of Barcelona. Fig. 2 illustrates the surface texture and shape of FA1, DMS-B and DMS-C aggre- gates after being analyzed by a Leica MZ 12 Stereomicroscope (DMS-A material was not considered since it was discarded in concrete production as will be shown la- ter). It is clear that the DMS-C is the ﬁnest material. The samples were also analyzed by a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Fig. 3 shows that the crushed limestone sand (FA1) presented an angular shape as described by Felekoglu [28], who states that the ﬁne limestone particles have a tendency to coagulate. As Fig. 3 illustrates, the DMS materials also showed an angular shape, with coagulation of ﬁnest mate- rial appearing in DMS-B. However, as ﬁgures (e) and (f) show, the DMS-C material showed low surface porosity. On the other hand, shell content was not observed in any of the samples. A similar absorption capacity of 1.8% was found for DMS-A and DMS-B, while the absorption capacity of DMS-C was 0.8%. The typical values given in the litera- ture [29] for marine aggregates indicate a ﬁne content between 51% and 80–95% and absorption usually between 0.8% and 2%. DMS-C presented the highest density and an absorption capacity lower than that of DMS-B. The mineralogical composition of DMS-A, DMS-B and DMS-C, determined by X- ray diffraction, is given in Table 4. The results revealed that the primary component of DMS was quartz (SiO 2) and the second component, calcite (CaCO 3). Exhaustive sampling was carried out in order to de

specimens was determined using a scale and then they were introduced into the drum machine. A rotation speed of 3.1–3.5 rad/s for 300 rotations was adopted for a period of time of about 10 min. The impact strength test used is an adaptation of the test for ﬁber reinforced concrete according to UNE-EN 83 514 speciﬁcations. In this case, four cylinder sam- ples with a diameter of 15 cm and a height of 6.5 cm were tested for each type of concrete. They were obtained from cylinder specimens of 15 /C215 cm cut in two pieces. The volume of permeable pores and absorption (ASTM C 642), sorptivity (UNE 83982) as well as the water penetration depth under pressure (UNE-EN 12390: 8-2001) were determined at the age of 28 days of moist curing. Cylindrical specimens with a diameter of 15 cm and a height of 15 cm were used for the last test. Tests of sorptivity and volume of permeable pores were carried out on cubic specimens of 10 cm. For sorptivity determination, the bottom face of the molded specimens was in contact with the water ﬂow. The cumulative water absorbed was recorded at different time intervals of up to 2 h by weighing the specimen after removing the surface water using a dampened tissue. Then the amount of water absorbed was calculated and normalized with respect to the cross-section area of the speci- mens. Sorptivity is the slope of the regression curve of the quantity of water ab- sorbed by a unit surface area versus the square root of the elapsed time from 10 min to 120 min. 3. Results 3.1. Fresh properties 3.1.1. Workability Table 10 shows the slump test results for all the concretes fabri- cated. Dry consistence was required for mixtures with values lower than 3 cm in the slump test. A variable amount of additive was em- ployed in the fabrication of all concretes in order to obtain homoge- neous mixtures with the required slump. It was veriﬁed that concretes made with low percentages of DMS obtained the most workable mixtures. According to Newman [11] the effect of the size fraction of aggregates on the workability of concrete made with sea-dredged aggregates will depend primarily on the size fraction of the sand and its uniformity. It would appear that, with sea- dredged material, the size fraction of the aggregates and the result- ing workability of concrete mixtures are dependent upon three conditions: the actual size fraction of the deposit (whether the sand is processed or blended with land-based material); the degree of drainage which occurs and the percentage of crushed material introduced. With respect to the effect of shell and other impurities, the author attests that the presence of small, normally accepted, percentages of coal, chalk or clay is unlikely to affect workability. No shell content was observed in any of the DMS used in this work. 3.2. Hardened properties 3.2.1. Physical properties 3.2.1.1. Density and absorption. According to the results presented in Table 11 , the physical properties of the concrete made with DMS material were similar to those of conventional concrete. The partial replacement of FA1 0/4 mm by DMS slightly reduced the density of concrete, and the concrete made with DMS-B obtained a lower density than DMS-C, according to the DMS material den- sity. Regardless of the replacement rate, the replacement of FA1 0/4 mm by DMS-B did not produce any signiﬁcant change in absorption, in comparison with RC. However, the replacement of FA1 0/4 mm by DMS-C produced a reduction in the absorption capacity o

compressive strength of the concretes made with it. According to Donza and Cabrera [35], the inﬂuence of ﬁne aggregates on the compressive strength is not signiﬁcant when the paste volume re- mains constant and the sand is free of dust (as it is shown above, in this case the crushed limestone sand had a higher amount of ﬁne material <63 lm). The adequate contributions of DMS material could be attributed to the particle strength and to the different sur- face texture and shape of the DMS particles (since the cement con- tent and w/c were similar), in accordance with the results obtained by Donza et al. [36]. It was detected that after 7 days of moist curing, concretes CB50% and CC50% (made with the highest percentage of DMS) achieved the results presented by the reference concrete RC (see Table 12 ). It seems that at this age the addition of up to 50% of DMS improved the compaction of the mixtures because the gaps of the granular skeleton might have been ﬁlled up with ﬁner mate- rial. However, after 28 days of curing, all concretes made with gradual incorporation of DMS (from 25% to up to 50%) exceeded the results of the RC (see Fig. 4 ). The evolution of the compressive strength through time from 7 to 28 days (see Table 13 ) revealed that the concretes made with 25% and 35% of DMS-B and DMS-C presented a high increase in compressive strength, probably due to the adsorbed water in the DMS material surface and the higher additive amount used, thus producing a later (retarding) cement reaction. From 7 to 28 days the highest rate of gain (34%) was obtained by the CC25% concrete. That effect was also observed in the study conducted by Columbia University [18]. 3.2.2.2. Elastic modulus. Slight variation was observed among the re- sults obtained for the elastic modulus with values around 41,700 MPa for the reference concrete RC (see Table 12 ). Concrete CC50% obtained values a 5% higher with regard to RC, followed by CB50%, CC35% and CB35%. These results were in accordance with the previously presented values found for the compressive strength. It is widely assumed that the elastic modulus of concrete depends on the relative volume and stiffness of the coarse aggregate [37]. According to Donza et al. [36], the inﬂuence of the ﬁne aggregates at early ages is closely related to the texture and shape of the ﬁne aggregates. 3.2.2.3. Flexural tensile and splitting tensile strength. As Table 12 shows, the ﬂexural and splitting tensile strength of the concretes made with DMS-B and DMS-C presented a variation lower than 10% in their results with respect to those of the reference concrete. In all concretes the same volume of coarse aggregates was used and a similar interfacial transition zone was expected to appear in all concretes. It is also important to mention that according to EHE [32] recommendations, the minimum ﬂexural tensile strength in concretes for harbor pavement requires 4 MPa, and all the con- cretes made with DMS exceeded the required value. Concretes made with DMS-B showed higher ﬂexural strength than RC; however, the values decreased when a higher percentage of DMS was used. The use of low percentages of DMS-B (probably due to its size fraction and ﬁneness) improved the mortar phase, but when the highest percentage was used, the strength was re- duced. This fact could be explained by the coagulation of the ﬁne particles present in DMS-B. The concretes made with 25% of DMS-C material obtained lower strength than RC. However, the use of

3.2.3. Durability properties The following sections discuss in detail each one of the tests performed in order to evaluate the inﬂuence of DMS material on the durability of mixtures fabricated with partial substitution of raw crushed sand. Table 14 summarizes the corresponding dura- bility properties of all concretes produced after 28 days of moist curing. Each of the presented values is the average of three data. 3.2.3.1. Accessible pores. The replacement of FA1 0/4 mm by DMS-B and DMS-C did not produce any signiﬁcant change in the accessible pores with regard to RC, regardless of the replacement rate. Com- paring the values obtained in this research work with those col- lected in Limeira et al. [22], a smaller volume of accessible pores was observed in the present work, probably due to the lower effec- tive w/c ratio adopted (0.44 against 0.49) and, consequently, the more dense cement paste obtained. The use of DMS material (which was ﬁner than crushed lime- stone sand FA1) in substitution of FA1, improved the particle pack- ing density of the cementitious system. Also, probably due to the coagulation tendency of FA1 and DMS-B, entrapment of very small capillary pores might have occurred and consequently concrete made with DMS-C achieved better properties, as observed by Fel- ekoglu [28]. The CC35% concrete obtained values of accessible pores an 8.5% lower than RC. 3.2.3.2. Sorptivity. Fig. 6 presents the volume of water absorbed per unit surface area versus the square root of time. From this ﬁgure a linear trend was obtained for each type of concrete, thus revealing sorptivity from the slope of these trend lines. It was observed that the slope of all the trend lines is sharper at the beginning of the process. All the concretes presented a similar evolution during the test. The values found in the literature [37,39] revealed that the initial surface absorption results showed no signiﬁcant differ- ence between mixtures with sea sand and the control mixtures made with natural sand. As shown in Table 14 , concrete CC35% had the lowest absorp- tion coefﬁcient (0.046 mm/min 1/2) and RC had the highest one (0.060 mm/min1/2) in accordance with the results found for acces- sible pores. As mentioned above, this can be related to the smaller size fraction of DMS, which improved the mortar phase of the con- cretes. Consequently, the accessibility of water was reduced in the concretes since all mixtures were produced with the same w/c ra- tio. It is also worth mentioning the probable absence of entrap- ment of very small capillary pores due to the lack of coagulation of ﬁnest aggregates. By comparing these values with the ones given by Limeira et al. [22], it can be concluded that the sorptivity pre- sented the same order of magnitude. According to Neville [40] the sorptivity values of all concretes produced in this research lead to very good concretes. In this study ( Fig. 6) sorptivity on concretes achieved values around 0.05 mm/min 1/2 in all cases. 3.2.3.3. Water penetration under pressure. The results found for water penetration depth under pressure ( Table 14 ) were almost the same for CB35%, CB50% and CC50%. The concrete CC35% had the lowest water penetration depth while RC reached the highest penetration depth in comparison with the rest of the concretes (in accordance with the obtained results of accessible pores and sorptivity). All resulting values were below the limits (an average of 3 cm and a maximum of 5 cm) required by the EHE for mass con- crete exposed to marine air environment ( Fig. 7 ). 3.2.3.4. Ultrasonic pulse velocity. The direct transmission method described in the standard was used for the measurement of the ultrasonic pulse velocity (UPV) in order to obtain the maximum intensity of pulse propagation. The values obtained ( Table 14 ) show that all the concretes made with DMS material obtained slightly lower results around 2.5% with respect to RC. The increase of DMS incorporation augmented the velocity of concretes CC, and all the concretes produced presented higher velocity than RC, except for CC35%. The incorporation of a ﬁner size fraction of DMS material in substitution of raw coarse sand is be- lieved to enhance the paste, which becomes less porous, thus speeding up ultrasonic pulse velocity. The following values of UPV proposed by Whitehurst [41] for concrete with a density of approximately 2400 kg/m 3 are considered to be excellent, good, doubtful, poor and very poor, respectively: 4500; 3500–4500; 3000–3500; 2000–3000; and less than 2000 m/ s. According to Jones and Gatﬁeld [42] the lowest value for a good quality of concrete is between 4100–4700 m/s. According to these data, all the concretes studied in this work had an excellent quality. 3.3. Relationship among properties The deterioration of concrete structures is usually caused by the penetration of external aggressive agents. Therefore, the durability of concrete is strongly related to the quality of the ﬁrst few centi- meters of material below the surface (coverage) [43]. The volume of accessible pores, as well as water penetration under pressure and sorptivity are considered as durability indices because they are useful to quantify the resistance of the materials against the penetration of external agents. For this reason they have been sub- jected to several studies [44,45], and in all cases direct relation- ships have been found among these properties, which means that if the porosity increases, so do the water penetration under pr

4. Conclusions According to the experimental program performed in this study with the partial replacement of FA1 0/4 mm by DMS from the Port of Barcelona, the following conclusions were reached: 4.1. DMS characterization /C15According to the physical characterization, the dredged marine sand (DMS) studied contained an amount of ﬁne material; in all of the DMS materials studied, more than 70% of the mate- rial passes through a # 250 lm sieve; however, the size of the aggregate could vary and consequently the size fraction deﬁnition of 0/4 mm and 0/0.5 mm was adopted in this study. /C15The DMS obtained from the surface layer of the disposal site at the Port of Barcelona contained a high amount of dust (its use in concrete was impossible). However, the DMS obtained from the hydraulic ﬁll site showed a low amount of dust. /C15All the DMS materials showed an angular shape and lower porosity than crushed limestone sand. /C15According to the mineralogical characterization, the DMS used had silica as primary component and calcite as secondary component. /C15A negligible contaminant level with values below the standard limits was observed. With respect to salt content, some samples exceeded the chloride limits. Consequently, if it will be used for concrete production, DMS must be mixed with raw aggregates in an adequate percentage in order to maintain the standard requirements. /C15DMS location has a direct inﬂuence on its composition, espe- cially with respect to the level of concentration of contami- nants. Systematic mixture of the stockpiles is strongly recommended prior to its use as construction material. 4.1.1. Fresh properties of concretes /C15The fresh properties of the concretes using DMS in partial sub- stitution of raw sand 0/4 mm were similar to those of the con- trol mixture, with the dry consistency required for the design of concrete pavements. /C15No inﬂuence of salt content on the behavior of the mixtures with respect to workability was observed. 4.2. Hardened properties of concretes /C15The presence of DMS in concretes caused a slight decrease in density, while absorption was maintained or reduced, in com- parison with RC, regardless of the replacement ratio. /C15Mechanical (compressive strength, tensile and ﬂexural tensile strength) and physical properties of the concretes made with DMS in substitution of crushed limestone sand 0/4 mm were similar to those of the reference concrete RC. /C15The ﬂexural tensile strength found in concretes made with DMS fulﬁlled the standard requirements of 4 MPa. /C15The presence of DMS slightly increased the abrasion resistance of concretes. 4.3. Durability properties of concretes /C15The use of DMS in substitution of raw sand maintained or reduced the accessible pores, the sorptivity and the water pen- etration depth under pressure. All concretes produced with DMS obtained adequate properties according to water penetra- tion depth under pressure for exposure to the marine environment. /C15All concrete specimens showed a high UPV after 28 days of curing. This study shows that dredged marine sand can be successfully used as a ﬁne aggregate for concrete production. The results indi- cate a good quality of concretes and, therefore, they verify the appropriate behavior of these materials in the short term in com- parison with the reference mixture. The main property required by the Spanish standard for port pavement construction was achieved. Therefore, it can be considered as an acceptable materi
